Ab main ek level aur upar hoon — I take photos of topper's notebook and zoom in during bus ride. Professional level copying.

Mujhe pata hai ye galat hai but agar homework itna boring hoga toh kya karun.

Biggest scam in Indian schools: "Marks don't matter."

Agar marks don't matter then why does my entire family know my percentage before I've even had breakfast?

I don't hate exams. I hate the drama around exams.

title: LinkedIn #1: "The Fraction Error That Changed Everything" — Building LearnX

content.instagram:
We were three months into building LearnX.

Our AI engine could grade answers instantly.

Fast. Accurate. Impressive demo.

Then we looked closer at the data.

61 lakh students answered 1/2 + 1/3 = 2/5.

Not a typo. Sixty-one lakh. Same wrong answer.

They weren't guessing. They were applying a rule.

A rule that made perfect sense to them:
add the tops, add the bottoms.

It's logical. It's consistent. It's completely wrong.

And no one caught it. Not in Class 5. Not in Class 6. Not in Class 7.

By Class 8, they'd decided they were "bad at Maths."

They weren't bad at Maths.
They had one broken concept from three years ago.

That night, we scrapped our grading engine.

We started building something different:
a system that doesn't just catch wrong answers.
It catches the wrong understanding behind them.

We called it the Mistake Genome.

It took 14 months longer than the grading engine would have.

But when a kid now gets a fraction wrong,
LearnX doesn't just say "incorrect."
It says: "I think you're adding numerators and denominators separately. Let's fix that."

That's the difference between an app that grades
and an app that teaches.

It started with a fraction.
It changed everything about how we build.

#EdTech #BuildingInPublic #LearnX #MistakeGenome #Education

title: LinkedIn #2: "Why We Made It Free (The Real Reason)" — Building LearnX

content.instagram:
People keep asking why LearnX is free.

They expect a clever business answer.
A freemium model. A data play. A long-term monetization strategy.

Here's the actual reason:

When we were testing our beta in a school in rural Rajasthan,
a girl asked if she could use the app at home.

We said yes.

She asked if it cost money.

We said we hadn't decided yet.

She nodded. Put the phone down. Didn't ask again.

Her teacher told us later:
"She assumed it would cost money.
Everything good always does.
She's learned not to want things she can't have."

She was 11 years old.

We went back to the office that night
and took the pricing model off the whiteboard.

It's been free since.

Not free as a growth hack.
Not free for now.
Free because the kid who can't pay
is not less intelligent than the kid who can.

And any system that gates learning behind a paywall
has already decided which children matter.

We decided all of them do.

That's it. That's the business model.

#FreeEducation #EdTech #LearnX #BuildingInPublic #EducationForAll

title: LinkedIn #3: "The Feature Nobody Asked For" — Building LearnX

content.instagram:
The most-used feature in LearnX wasn't in our product roadmap.

It wasn't the AI tutor.
Not the brain profiler.
Not the revision scheduler.

It was a button.

A small button at the bottom of a practice test result screen.

"Share with Parents."

A developer added it in a Friday sprint
because his daughter kept showing him her school drawings
and he thought, "kids want someone to see."

We almost removed it in the next sprint.
"Not a core feature."
"Adds UI clutter."
"Parents can just check the dashboard."

Then the data came in.

9 lakh taps in one year.

Kids sharing 60% scores. 45% scores. 90% scores.
Not just wins. Everything.

They weren't sharing results.
They were saying: "See me. I'm trying."

We made the button bigger.
We made it more prominent.
We stopped calling it a "sharing feature" internally.

We call it what it is:
the moment a child asks to be seen.

That developer's daughter is 9 now.
She still shows him her drawings.
He still stops everything and looks.

#ProductBuilding #EdTech #LearnX #UserExperience #BuildingInPublic

title: LinkedIn #4: "What Kota Taught Us" — Building LearnX

content.instagram:
The city with the highest LearnX usage per student is Kota, Rajasthan.

Let that sink in for a second.

The coaching capital of India.
Where families spend 2-5 lakh per year on tuition.
Where 1.5 lakh students migrate every year to study.

They're also using a free app. More than anyone else.

We went to Kota to understand why.

Here's what students told us:

"The coaching class teaches everyone the same way.
LearnX knows where I'm stuck."

"I can't ask my doubt in a 400-person batch.
I can ask it here."

"My coaching is for JEE.
But I'm also failing in school because nobody teaches me school syllabus anymore."

One student said something I haven't been able to forget:

"Sir, I came to Kota because my parents believe in me.
I use LearnX because I need to believe in myself."

We're not against coaching.
We're not replacing coaching.

We're just building for the kid
who sits in the back of a 400-person room
and can't raise their hand.

They can raise their hand with us.

#Kota #EdTech #LearnX #IndianEducation #BuildingInPublic

content.instagram:
The most dangerous sentence in Indian parenting:

"My child is average."

Not because it's harsh.
Because it's almost always wrong.

Average at what?
Measured how?
Compared to whom?
On which day?
In which format?
Under how much pressure?

A child who scores 55% on a written Maths exam
might be extraordinary at spatial reasoning.

A child who "can't focus in class"
might have the highest interpersonal intelligence in the room.

A child who "isn't academic"
might be a kinesthetic learner
forced to sit still for 6 hours a day
in a system designed for one type of brain.

"Average" isn't a diagnosis.
It's a failure of measurement.

Howard Gardner identified 9 types of intelligence in 1983.
It's 2026.

Most report cards still measure 2.

The problem was never your child.
The problem is the lens.

#Education #MultipleIntelligences #Parenting #EdTech #EveryChildShines

content.instagram:
Here's something most students and parents don't know:

Only 11% of students revisit a topic
after learning it the first time.

Not 11% on LearnX. 11% everywhere.
That's a well-documented cognitive science finding.

Your child learns photosynthesis on Tuesday.
By Friday, 70% of it is gone.
By next month, it might as well have never happened.

This is the Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve.
Published in 1885.
141 years ago.

And yet our entire education system
is still built on the "teach once, test later" model.

We teach a chapter.
We move to the next chapter.
We never come back.

Then we're shocked when students "forget everything"
right before the exam.

They didn't forget because they're lazy.
They forgot because that's what brains do.

The solution isn't "study harder."
The solution is spaced repetition:
revisiting the same concept at increasing intervals
until it moves from short-term to long-term memory.

At LearnX, we built this into the product.
R1 to R10 — ten revision cycles
automatically scheduled across the year.

Not because we're clever.
Because a scientist figured this out in 1885
and the system still hasn't caught up.

#SpacedRepetition #LearningScience #EdTech #Education #CognitiveScience

title: LinkedIn #7: "Confidence Is a Skill" — Thought Leadership

content.instagram:
We found something in our data that stopped us cold.

38% of students on LearnX
selected the correct answer first
and then changed it to the wrong one.

Read that again.

They KNEW the right answer.
Then they doubted themselves.
And chose wrong.

This wasn't a knowledge gap.
It was a confidence gap.

The student who knows Canberra is the capital of Australia
but changes it to Sydney
because "that doesn't sound right"
doesn't need more studying.

They need to learn to trust what they already know.

We don't talk about this enough in education.

We measure correctness.
We don't measure conviction.

A child who gets 80% with full confidence
is in a very different place
than a child who gets 80%
but second-guessed every single answer.

Same score. Completely different learner.

At LearnX, we now track confidence alongside correctness.

Because getting it right
and knowing you got it right
are two very different things.

And the second one matters more in real life
than any exam will ever capture.

#Confidence #Education #LearningScience #EdTech #StudentWellbeing

title: LinkedIn #8: "The Report Card Problem" — Thought Leadership

content.instagram:
A report card is a photograph.

One frame.
One angle.
One moment in time.

It captures what a child remembered
on a specific day,
in a specific format,
under maximum stress,
in a room full of other stressed children,
with a time limit,
after potentially sleeping badly the night before.

And we treat this photograph
as if it's the entire movie.

"She got 72% in Science. She's average."

Is she, though?

What about the week she spent building a working circuit at home?
What about the documentary she watched three times because she was fascinated by black holes?
What about the fact that she can explain ecosystems to her younger brother better than most teachers?

None of that shows up on a report card.

A report card measures recall under pressure.
It doesn't measure curiosity.
It doesn't measure persistence.
It doesn't measure understanding.

I'm not saying report cards are useless.
I'm saying they're incomplete.

And making life-altering decisions
about a child's potential
based on incomplete information
is something we should talk about more.

#Education #Assessment #Parenting #ReportCards #EdTech

content.instagram:
2,84,19,000.

That's the number of questions solved on LearnX last month.

I want to write something inspiring about this number.
Something about "the future of education"
and "transforming learning at scale."

But honestly?

I just keep thinking about what 2.84 crore questions looks like
at the individual level.

It's a kid in Patna doing fractions at 6 AM before school.
It's a girl in Kota practicing between coaching classes.
It's a boy in Kerala doing his 7th revision cycle on a topic he used to fail.
It's someone in a village I'll probably never visit
using a phone they share with their sibling
to study something that might change their life.

2.84 crore questions.
Zero rupees charged.
No premium tier. No paywall. No "upgrade to unlock."

When people ask how we measure success at LearnX,
this is what I think about.

Not the number.
The 2.84 crore individual moments behind it.

Each one is a child choosing to learn
when they could have done literally anything else.

That's not a metric. That's trust.

And we don't take it lightly.

#EdTech #LearnX #FreeEducation #Milestone #HarBacchaStar

content.instagram:
We hit a quiet milestone this week.

9,12,447 students tapped the "Share with Parents" button this year.

I wasn't going to post about it.

It's not a funding round.
It's not a download number.
It's not a celebrity endorsement.

It's just kids pressing a button.

But I can't stop thinking about what that button means.

A 12-year-old finishes a practice test at 11 PM.
Scores 67%.
Not great. Not terrible.
And still hits "Share with Parents."

Why?

Because the score isn't the point.
The effort is the point.
And they want someone to know they tried.

9 lakh kids said "Look what I did"
to the people whose opinion matters most to them.

Some of those parents saw the notification immediately.
Some saw it the next morning.
Some probably never saw it.

But the kid pressed the button anyway.

That's not a product metric.
That's a child saying: I'm here. I'm trying. See me.

We didn't design that button to go viral.
We didn't design it to be a feature.

We designed it because a developer on our team
remembered what it felt like
to want someone to notice.

9 lakh taps.
9 lakh moments of "look, I did something."

This is why we build.

#EdTech #LearnX #ProductBuilding #BuildingInPublic #EveryChildShines
